hausa.name: Hausa
hausa.description: One of the largest languages in Africa by speaker count, Hausa is a lingua franca across much of northern Nigeria, Niger, and the wider Sahel. It is written in both a Latin-based script (Boko) and an Arabic-based script (Ajami).

yoruba.name: Yoruba
yoruba.description: A tonal language spoken across south-western Nigeria and closely tied to Yoruba religion, literature, and film (Nollywood). It has large diaspora-speaking communities in the Americas via Yoruba-derived traditions such as Santería and Candomblé.

igbo.name: Igbo
igbo.description: A tonal language of south-eastern Nigeria with numerous dialects; a standardized literary form is used in schools and media. Igbo has a long documented literary tradition, including Chinua Achebe's foundational English-language novels drawing on Igbo culture.

fulfulde.name: Fulfulde
fulfulde.description: The language of the Fulani people, historically associated with cattle-herding and pastoralism, spoken across a wide belt of West and Central Africa. Nigeria hosts one of the largest Fulfulde-speaking populations, concentrated in the north.

kanuri.name: Kanuri
kanuri.description: Spoken mainly around Borno and Yobe states, Kanuri is the language of the historic Kanem-Bornu Empire, one of the longest-lived states in African history. It stands apart from most of its Nigerian neighbors as a Nilo-Saharan rather than Niger-Congo or Afro-Asiatic language.

tiv.name: Tiv
tiv.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Tivoid
tiv.description: Spoken mainly in Benue State and parts of Taraba and Nasarawa, Tiv is part of the Tivoid group, a branch related to the wider Bantoid family. The Tiv are one of Nigeria's largest ethnic groups outside the 'big three' (Hausa, Yoruba, Igbo).

ibibio.name: Ibibio
ibibio.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Cross River > Delta-Cross > Lower Cross
ibibio.description: Spoken across Akwa Ibom State, Ibibio is closely related to Annang and Efik, and the three are sometimes described as a single dialect continuum. It has a rich tradition of proverbs and masquerade performance central to Ibibio cultural life.

efik.name: Efik
efik.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Cross River > Delta-Cross > Lower Cross
efik.description: Centered on Calabar in Cross River State, Efik was historically a major trade language of the Niger Delta coast and was among the first Nigerian languages to be reduced to writing by 19th-century missionaries.

annang.name: Annang
annang.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Cross River > Delta-Cross > Lower Cross
annang.description: Closely related to Ibibio and often mutually intelligible with it, Annang is spoken in the western part of Akwa Ibom State and is associated with a strong local tradition of yam festivals and proverbs.

oron.name: Oron
oron.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Cross River > Delta-Cross > Lower Cross
oron.description: Spoken by the Oro people in coastal Akwa Ibom State. Though part of the Lower Cross branch and neighboring the Ibibio, Oron speakers maintain a distinct linguistic and cultural identity.

obolo.name: Obolo (Andoni)
obolo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Cross River > Delta-Cross > Lower Cross
obolo.description: Spoken in the coastal borderlands of Rivers and Akwa Ibom states. Obolo is unique among Lower Cross languages for its extensive contact with and lexical influences from the Ijoid group due to its maritime history.

edo.name: Edo (Bini)
edo.description: The language of the historic Benin Kingdom, centered on Benin City in Edo State. Its Edoid family relatives are spread across the wider Edo State and Delta region.

esan.name: Esan
esan.description: Spoken in the Esan (Ishan) region of Edo State, this Edoid language shares deep historical ties with the neighboring Benin Kingdom while retaining a distinct identity and dialect cluster.

urhobo.name: Urhobo
urhobo.description: Spoken across Delta State, Urhobo is closely related to Isoko, with which it forms a tight dialect cluster within the Edoid family. Urhobo culture is closely tied to the Niger Delta's fishing and farming economy.

isoko.name: Isoko
isoko.description: Closely related to Urhobo and spoken in the southern part of Delta State, Isoko split from a common ancestor language with Urhobo relatively recently by linguistic standards.

okpe.name: Okpe
okpe.description: Spoken in Delta State, Okpe is an Southwestern Edoid language distinct from neighboring Urhobo despite high cultural integration. It features a unique vowel system that attracts significant linguistic interest.

itsekiri.name: Itsekiri
itsekiri.description: Spoken in Delta and parts of Ondo State around Warri, Itsekiri is part of the Yoruboid group and shows strong historical links to Yoruba, alongside centuries of contact with Portuguese traders and neighboring Edoid and Ijoid groups.

igala.name: Igala
igala.description: Spoken in Kogi State around the confluence of the Niger and Benue rivers, Igala is closely related to Yoruba within the Yoruboid group and is the language of the historic Igala Kingdom.

ijaw.name: Ijaw (Izon)
ijaw.description: Ijaw (most widely represented by the Izon variety) belongs to Ijoid, a branch of Niger-Congo distinct enough that some linguists treat it as one of the family's earliest splits. Ijaw speakers are concentrated across the Niger Delta's riverine communities in Bayelsa, Delta, and Rivers states.

kalabari.name: Kalabari
kalabari.description: Spoken in Rivers State, Kalabari is a prominent Eastern Ijoid variety historically tied to the powerful Kalabari Kingdom, which was a core trading partner with Europeans in the pre-colonial oil palm and mercantile trade.

ikwerre.name: Ikwerre
ikwerre.description: Spoken around Port Harcourt in Rivers State, Ikwerre is part of the Igboid group, closely related to Igbo, though its speakers maintain a distinct ethnic identity from the wider Igbo-speaking area.

ukwuani.name: Ukwuani (Ukwuani-Aboh-Ndoni)
ukwuani.description: Spoken primarily in western Delta State and parts of Rivers State, Ukwuani is an Igboid language featuring high mutual intelligibility with standardized Igbo but possessing distinct lexical variations shaped by Delta regional history.

ogoni.name: Ogoni (Khana)
ogoni.description: Khana, the largest Ogoni language, is spoken in Rivers State and forms its own small Ogonoid branch within the Cross River languages. The Ogoni people gained international attention through 1990s environmental and civil-rights activism led by Ken Saro-Wiwa.

nupe.name: Nupe
nupe.description: Spoken mainly in Niger and Kwara states along the Niger River, Nupe is the namesake of the Nupoid group and the language of the historic Nupe Kingdom, known for its riverine trade and craftsmanship.

gbagyi.name: Gbagyi (Gwari)
gbagyi.description: Spoken across Niger State, the Federal Capital Territory, and parts of Kaduna, Gbagyi is one of the indigenous languages of the Abuja area and is well known for its pottery-making tradition.

ebira.name: Ebira
ebira.description: Centered on Kogi State, particularly the Okene area, Ebira belongs to the Nupoid group and is known for the Eku Masquerade Festival and a strong tradition of woven textiles.

idoma.name: Idoma
idoma.description: Spoken in southern Benue State, Idoma anchors the small Idomoid group and shares the Benue River region with its neighbor, Tiv, despite the two languages belonging to different subfamilies.

igede.name: Igede
igede.description: Spoken in southern Benue State around Otukpo, Igede is part of the Idomoid group and closely related to Idoma, though speakers regard themselves as ethnically distinct.

agatu.name: Agatu
agatu.description: Closely related to Idoma and spoken predominantly in northern Benue State and southern Nasarawa State, Agatu forms a critical part of the broader Idomoid linguistic cluster.

berom.name: Berom
berom.description: The largest of the many Plateau languages native to Plateau State, Berom is centered on the Jos area, a region famous for extraordinary linguistic diversity packed into a small geographic space.

eggon.name: Eggon
eggon.description: Spoken in Nasarawa State, Eggon belongs to the Plateau branch of Benue-Congo, sharing distant roots with languages like Berom further north on the Jos Plateau.

tarok.name: Tarok
tarok.description: Spoken primarily in the southeastern portion of Plateau State, Tarok belongs to the Plateau branch of languages. The Tarok people have a distinct martial tradition heavily integrated into Nigeria's historical military culture.

mada.name: Mada
mada.description: Spoken in Nasarawa and southern Kaduna states, Mada is a prominent member of the Plateau linguistic branch and anchors numerous central-belt farming communities.

ngas.name: Ngas (Angas)
ngas.description: Spoken in Plateau State, Ngas represents the Chadic-language presence on the otherwise Niger-Congo-dominated Jos Plateau, illustrating the deep linguistic mixing of the region.

mupun.name: Mupun
mupun.description: Spoken in Plateau State, Mupun is closely related to Ngas. It has been heavily studied for its unique grammatical interactions with grammatical aspect and tone systems.

jukun.name: Jukun (Wapan)
jukun.description: Spoken across Taraba State, Jukun anchors the Jukunoid group and is the language of the historic Kwararafa confederacy, a powerful precolonial state in the Benue valley.

mumuye.name: Mumuye
mumuye.description: The largest native language of Taraba State, Mumuye belongs to the structurally diverse Adamawa branch. The Mumuye people are known for a decentralized traditional political system and distinct sculptural art.

bura.name: Bura-Pabir
bura.description: Spoken in Adamawa and Borno states, Bura belongs to the Central Chadic group and has one of the longer traditions of Christian mission-related literacy among Chadic languages in the region.

marghi.name: Marghi (Margi)
marghi.description: Spoken primarily in Borno and Adamawa states near the Cameroon border. Marghi is a cornerstone language of the Central Chadic group and has a well-documented linguistic literature analyzing its complex consonant clusters.

kamwe.name: Kamwe
kamwe.description: Spoken primarily around the Mandara Mountains region of Adamawa State. Kamwe boasts a diverse collection of highly local dialects and is historically tied to defensive highland terraced-farming communities.

tangale.name: Tangale
tangale.description: Spoken in Gombe State, Tangale gives its name to the Tangale-Waja group within West Chadic and is known for a distinctive tonal system extensively studied by linguists.

waja.name: Waja
waja.description: Spoken in Gombe State, Waja neighbors Tangale. Despite sharing heavy geographic and cultural traits with the Chadic-speaking Tangale, Waja belongs firmly to the Niger-Congo family tree.

ngizim.name: Ngizim
ngizim.description: Spoken in Yobe State, Ngizim belongs to the Bade-Ngizim group of West Chadic and has been steadily losing ground to Hausa and Kanuri as regional linguae francae.

bade.name: Bade
bade.description: Spoken in Yobe and Jigawa states, Bade co-anchors the Bade-Ngizim language cluster and retains a traditional political structure led by the Emir of Bade.

shuwa-arabic.name: Shuwa Arabic
shuwa-arabic.description: A distinct variety of Arabic spoken in Borno State and across the Lake Chad basin. It serves as a major trade and pastoral language of the far northeast, showing heavy lexical convergence with Kanuri.

bekwarra.name: Bekwarra
bekwarra.description: Spoken in northern Cross River State, Bekwarra belongs to the small Bendi branch. It has achieved high rates of local literacy and was documented heavily via early regional bible translation work.

bette-bendi.name: Bette-Bendi
bette-bendi.description: Spoken around Obudu in northern Cross River State, this language community is famously situated near the Cameroon border highlands and exhibits a complex noun-class framework.

egun.name: Egun (Gbe)
egun.description: Spoken in Badagry (Lagos State) and Ogun State along the Benin border. It is a Gbe language closely tied to Fon and Aja in neighboring Benin, representing a distinct precolonial migration along the Atlantic coast.

nigerian-pidgin.name: Nigerian Pidgin
nigerian-pidgin.description: An English-lexified creole spoken as a lingua franca across Nigeria, especially in Lagos and the Niger Delta, with growing use in music, film, and everyday conversation across ethnic lines. Unlike the other languages here it isn't tied to a single ethnic group, functioning instead as a shared bridge language.

abanyom.name: Abanyom
abanyom.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Ekoid
abanyom.description: Also known as Bakor, Abanyom is spoken by the Abanyom people in Cross River State and belongs to the Ekoid group, making it fairly closely related to the Bantu languages. It is a tonal language with the noun class system typical of Niger-Congo.

abon.name: Abon
abon.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Tivoid
abon.description: Abon, also rendered Abo, is a small Tivoid language of Nigeria, placing it in the same Southern Bantoid branch as Tiv.

abua.name: Abua
abua.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Central Delta
abua.description: Abua, also known as Abuan, is a Central Delta language spoken in the Niger Delta region of Nigeria.

abureni.name: Abureni
abureni.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Central Delta
abureni.description: Abureni is a Central Delta language of Nigeria's Niger Delta region, grouped with Ijoid-area languages in the same branch as Abua.

adim.name: Adim
adim.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River > Gwune
adim.description: Adim (Dim) is one of several dialect varieties within the Gwune dialect cluster, an Upper Cross River language of Nigeria spoken by the Akunakuna people, alongside related varieties such as Abayongo, Abini, Orum, Erei, and Etono.

aduge.name: Okpamheri
aduge.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Volta-Niger > Edoid
aduge.description: Okpamheri, also called Opameri, is an Edoid language of Nigeria; by 1973 it had only around 30,000 speakers, and the language is considered moribund today.

afade.name: Afade
afade.description: Afade is an Afro-Asiatic language of the Chadic branch spoken in eastern Nigeria and northwestern Cameroon.

afrike.name: Putukwam
afrike.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Bendi
afrike.description: Putukwam, also known as Utugwang-Irungene-Afrike, is a Bendi language spoken in Obudu Local Government Area of Cross River State, Nigeria.

akpes.name: Akpes
akpes.description: Akpes is an endangered language spoken by about 7,000 people in northern Ondo State, in an area where Yoruba serves as the lingua franca; the language is gradually giving way to Yoruba in everyday informal use.

alago.name: Alago
alago.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Idomoid > Akweya > Etulo-Idoma
alago.description: Alago, or Idoma Nokwu, is an Idomoid language spoken in Nigeria whose people were originally one with the Idoma of Benue State before splitting off around 1200 AD; the Alago remain a largely agrarian community growing crops such as yam, maize, and millet.

atsam.name: Atsam
atsam.description: Atsam, known to the Hausa as Chawai, is a Kainji language spoken by the Atsam people mainly in Kauru LGA of Kaduna State and Bassa LGA of Plateau State in Nigeria's Middle Belt.

atyap.name: Atyap
atyap.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Central Plateau
atyap.description: The Atyap are a Middle Belt ethnic group of southern Kaduna and Plateau States who speak Tyap, one of the Central Plateau languages; their Hausa exonym is Kataf or Katab.

ayu.name: Ayu
ayu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Ninzic
ayu.description: Ayu is a minor, endangered Plateau language of southern Kaduna State in Nigeria's Middle Belt, tentatively classified among the Ninzic languages; it is no longer being reliably passed on to children.

bachama.name: Bachama
bachama.description: Bachama is a Chadic language spoken by the Bwatiye people of Adamawa State, Nigeria, principally in the Numan, Demsa, and Lamurde local government areas, with related varieties such as Basa Pwa and Basa Voti.

barke.name: Mburku
barke.description: Mburku, whose speakers prefer the name Burku, is a West Chadic language spoken by an estimated 7,000 to 10,000 people in the Miya District of Ganjuwa LGA, Bauchi State, Nigeria, and is not considered endangered.

basa-komo.name: Basa-Benue
basa-komo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji > Basa
basa-komo.description: Basa-Komo, part of the Basa-Benue Kainji language cluster of central Nigeria, is spoken around Bassa, Ankpa, and Nasarawa; speakers of this cluster often also speak Igala, Ebira, or Nupe.

basa-nge.name: Basa-Benue
basa-nge.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji > Basa
basa-nge.description: Basa-Nge, also known as Bassa Nupe, is a variety within the Basa-Benue Kainji language cluster spoken in central Nigeria near Bassa, Ankpa, Nasarawa, Gurara, Kwali, and Makurdi, whose speakers often also use Igala, Ebira, or Nupe.

bele.name: Ɓeele
bele.classification: Afroasiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
bele.description: Ɓeele, also known as Bele or Bellawa, is an endangered Afro-Asiatic language of the Bole-Tangale group spoken in a small number of villages in Bauchi State, Nigeria.

bina.name: Bina
bina.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
bina.description: Bina, known to the Hausa as Binawa, is a Kainji language of Nigeria within the East Kainji subgroup.

biseni.name: Biseni
biseni.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kwa > Ijaw
biseni.description: Biseni, also called Buseni, is one of the coastal Ijaw languages of the Niger Delta in Nigeria, and is not fully mutually intelligible with Inland Ijaw varieties.

boki.name: Bokyi
boki.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Bendi
boki.description: Bokyi is a regionally important Bendi language spoken by the Bokyi people of northern Cross River State, Nigeria, ranking among the fifteen most-spoken of the country's roughly 520 languages, with major dialects including Abu, Irruan, Osokom, and Wula.

bokkos.name: Ron
bokkos.classification: Afroasiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Ron
bokkos.description: Bokkos is a dialect within the Ron language cluster, an Afro-Asiatic Chadic group spoken in Plateau State, Nigeria, alongside related dialects such as Daffo-Mbar-Butura and Monguna.

boko.name: Boko
boko.description: Boko, also called Boo, is a Mande language spoken across Benin and Nigeria.

bokyi.name: Bokyi
bokyi.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Bendi
bokyi.description: Bokyi is a regionally important Bendi language spoken by the Bokyi people of northern Cross River State, Nigeria, ranking among the fifteen most-spoken of the country's roughly 520 languages, with major dialects including Abu, Irruan, Osokom, and Wula.

bole.name: Bole
bole.description: Bole, also known as Bolanchi or Bolewa, is a West Chadic language spoken by around 250,000 people in Nigeria, including the Bara and Fika dialects of the Fika Emirate, and is written in the Latin alphabet.

adara.name: Adara
adara.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Central Plateau
adara.description: Adara, also known as Eda or Kadara, is a Central Plateau language spoken by the Adara people of Kaduna and Niger States, Nigeria; the name Adara also refers to the ethnic group itself, whose roughly 500,000 people are mostly Christian with a Muslim minority.

afo.name: Eloyi
afo.description: Eloyi, also known as Afo or Ajiri, is a Plateau language of uncertain finer classification spoken by the Eloyi people across Agatu and Otukpo LGAs of Benue State and parts of Nasarawa State, Nigeria.

ahan.name: Ahan
ahan.description: Ahan, also spelled Aahan, is a highly divergent Volta-Niger language of Nigeria with no close relatives among the languages documented here.

akuku.name: Akuku
akuku.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Volta-Niger > Edoid
akuku.description: Akuku is an Edoid language of Nigeria, placing it in the same branch as Edo and other related languages of the Edo-speaking region.

amo.name: Amo
amo.description: Amo, also known as Map or Timap, is a divergent Kainji language of Nigeria.

anaguta.name: Guta
anaguta.description: The Guta language, also recorded as Iguta or Naraguta, is a Plateau language of Nigeria spoken by the Guta people, who number between 10,000 and 12,000.

ankwa.name: Iku
ankwa.description: Iku, also known as Iku-Gora-Ankwa or Ekhwa, is a Plateau language of Nigeria.

arum.name: Alumu
arum.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Alumic
arum.description: Alumu is a Plateau language spoken by around 7,000 people in Nasarawa State, Nigeria, notable for having lost the nominal affix system that is otherwise characteristic of the Niger-Congo family.

asholio.name: Tyap
asholio.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Central Plateau > Tyapic
asholio.description: Asholio, or Sholyio, is one of the named dialectal varieties within the Tyap cluster, a regionally important group of Plateau languages in Nigeria's Middle Belt also known by the Hausa exonym Katab or Kataf.

aten.name: Eten
aten.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Beromic
aten.description: Eten, also spelled Iten, is a Plateau language spoken by the Niten people in the town of Ganawuri in Plateau State, Nigeria.

baushi.name: Bauchi
baushi.description: Bauchi, also spelled Bauci or Baushi, is a cluster of Kainji languages spoken in Rafi LGA of Niger State, Nigeria.

burak.name: Burak
burak.description: Burak is an Adamawa language spoken by the Burak people in Nigeria, centered on the village of Burak (known locally as "Tiire") in the northern foothills of the Chonge-Mona Range, with a small population historically numbering under 5,000.

buta.name: Gamo-Ningi
buta.description: Gamo (also called Buta) and Ningi form an apparently extinct Kainji dialect cluster of Nigeria.

chip.name: Miship
chip.description: Miship, also known as Chip, is an Afro-Asiatic language spoken in Plateau State, Nigeria, with Doka as one of its dialects alongside the Longmaar and Jiḇaam varieties.

chokobo.name: Zora
chokobo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
chokobo.description: Zora, also called Izora or Cokoba in Hausa, is a Kainji language of Nigeria belonging to the East Kainji subgroup.

cibak.name: Cibak
cibak.description: Cibak (also rendered Chibok or Chibbak) is a Chadic language spoken by about 200,000 mostly Kibaku people in the Askira/Uba, Chibok, and Damboa areas of southern Borno State; most speakers are Christian, and the schoolgirls abducted in the 2014 Chibok kidnapping were largely Cibak-speaking.

cipu.name: Cipu
cipu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > Shingini languages
cipu.description: Cicipu is a Kainji language spoken by about 20,000 people, who call themselves Acipu (Acipawa in Hausa), in northwest Nigeria, and is notable for a complex noun class system along with lexical and grammatical tone.

cori.name: Kyoli
cori.description: Kyoli, also known as Cori or Chori, is a Plateau language spoken in southern Kaduna State, Nigeria.

dadiya.name: Dadiya
dadiya.description: Dadiya (also called Loodiya) is one of the Savanna languages of northeastern Nigeria, spoken by communities in Balanga LGA of Gombe State and in parts of Adamawa and Taraba states.

doemak.name: Pan
doemak.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Angas > Sura-Gerka > Kofyar
doemak.description: Pan, also known as Doemak, is an Afro-Asiatic dialect cluster spoken in Plateau State, Nigeria, classified within the Kofyar group of West Chadic languages.

doka.name: Doka
doka.description: Doka, called Ejuele in Adara, is a Plateau language of Nigeria.

duguri.name: Jarawa (Nigeria)
duguri.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
duguri.description: Jarawa, also called Jhar or Jaranci in Hausa, is the most populous of the Jarawan Bantu languages of northern Nigeria and consists of a dialect cluster with many varieties.

duka.name: Hun-Saare
duka.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > Northwest Kainji
duka.description: Hun-Saare, also known as Duka, is a Kainji language of Nigeria whose eastern and western dialects are called Hun and Saare, though speakers commonly use "Saare" for both.

dulbu.name: Dulbu
dulbu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
dulbu.description: Dulbu is one of the Jarawan Bantu languages of Nigeria, whose speakers are increasingly shifting to Hausa.

dungu.name: Dungu
dungu.description: Dungu, also known as Dingi, is a Kainji-area language of Nigeria.

dza.name: Jen
dza.description: Dza, also called Jen, is an Adamawa language of Nigeria spoken along the northern bank of the Benue River in the border regions of Gombe, Adamawa, and Taraba states.

ekajuk.name: Kajuk
ekajuk.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Ekoid
ekajuk.description: Ekajuk, also spelled Akajo or Akajuk, is an Ekoid language of the Niger-Congo family spoken in Cross River State and surrounding areas of Nigeria; the Ekajuk are among several peoples in the region who use the nsibidi ideographic writing system.

eket.name: Ekid
eket.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Ibibio
eket.description: Ekid, also called Esit Eket, is a lower Cross River language of Nigeria closely related to Ibibio varieties, tracing its origins to ancestral migrations of Ibibio-related peoples from the Cameroon highlands who settled fishing and farming communities in present-day Akwa Ibom State.

ekoi.name: Ekoi
ekoi.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Ekoid
ekoi.description: The Ekoi, also known as Ejagham, are an ethnic group of southeastern Nigeria and southwestern Cameroon whose related communities include the Etung and groups in Ikom and Ogoja; they have long lived alongside the neighboring Biase, Efik, Annang, and Ibibio peoples.

emai-iuleha-ora.name: Ivbiosakon
emai-iuleha-ora.description: Ivbiosakon, also called Aoma, is an Edoid language of Edo State, Nigeria, with Ora and Emai used as alternate dialect names.

engenni.name: Engenni
engenni.description: Engenni, also spelled Eṣeṇe, is an Edoid language of Nigeria, and Engenni migrants historically expanded into the neighboring Epie-Atissa area near Yenagoa in Bayelsa State.

enwan.name: Enwan
enwan.description: Enwan is an Edoid language spoken in Edo State, Nigeria.

epie-atissa.name: Epie-Atissa
epie-atissa.description: The Epie and Atissa are two related peoples living along Epie Creek northeast of Yenagoa in Bayelsa State, Nigeria, whose combined community grew with an influx of Edoid-speaking Engenni migrants who share cultural ties with the neighboring Ijaw.

etsako.name: Yekhee
etsako.description: Yekhee, also called Afenmai or Iyekhe, is an Edoid language spoken in Edo State, Nigeria, by the Afenmai people, some of whom instead use the district name Etsako; it was formerly labeled "Kukuruku" by British colonial administrators, a name now considered derogatory.

etung.name: Ejagham
etung.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Ekoid
etung.description: Ejagham, also known as Jagham or Ekoi, is an Ekoid language spoken by the Ekoi people across Nigeria and Cameroon; the Ekoi are among the peoples associated with creating the Nsibidi ideographic script.

etuno.name: Ebira
etuno.description: Ebira is a Nupoid language spoken by around 2 million people in Nigeria's Middle Belt, and is the most divergent Nupoid language, with the Okene variety serving as its central dialect.

fyam.name: Pyem
fyam.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > East Plateau
fyam.description: Pyem, rendered Fyam or Fyem in Hausa, is a Plateau language spoken by the people of Pyem Chiefdom, headquartered in Gindiri, and is one of the indigenous languages of Mangu LGA, Plateau State, Nigeria.

fyer.name: Fyer
fyer.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Ron
fyer.description: Fyer, also known as Fier, is a West Chadic language spoken in Plateau State, Nigeria.

gaanda.name: Ga'anda
gaanda.description: Ga'anda, also known as Ganda or Ga'andu, is a Biu-Mandara Chadic language spoken by about 43,000 people in the Gombi LGA of Adamawa State, Nigeria, with three dialects: Ga'anda, Gabun, and Boga.

gade.name: Gade
gade.description: Gade is an independent Gadoid language of Nigeria; recent classification treats it as its own branch of Benue-Congo rather than as a Nupoid language, despite being spoken by the Gade people.

galambi.name: Galambu
galambi.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
galambi.description: Galambu, also known as Galambi or Galambe, is a Chadic language spoken in Nigeria, though most members of the Galambu ethnic group no longer speak it.

gamergu.name: Wandala
gamergu.description: Wandala, also known as Mandara or Mura, is a Chadic language of the Afro-Asiatic family spoken across the Nigeria-Cameroon border.

ganawuri.name: Eten
ganawuri.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > Beromic
ganawuri.description: Eten, also called Iten, is a Plateau language of Nigeria spoken by the Niten people of the town of Ganawuri in Plateau State.

gbiri-niragu.name: Gbiri-Niragu
gbiri-niragu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
gbiri-niragu.description: Gbiri-Niragu, also called Gure-Kahugu, is a Kainji language of Nigeria whose Tugbiri variety is spoken around the village of Gure in Lere LGA of southern Kaduna State, with Aniragu speakers living just to the north; speakers are shifting toward Hausa.

geji.name: Geji
geji.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > South Bauchi
geji.description: Geji, also called Gezawa, is a minor Chadic dialect cluster of Bauchi State, Nigeria, comprising three similar varieties: Buu, Gyaazi, and Məgang.

gengle.name: Kugama
gengle.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Adamawa-Ubangi > Leko-Nimbari > Mumuye-Yendang
gengle.description: Kugama, also known as Wam or Gengle, is an Adamawa language of the Yendang group spoken in the Mayo-Belwa and Fufore local government areas of Adamawa State, Nigeria.

gera.name: Gera
gera.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
gera.description: Gera, also known as Gerawa or Fyandigeri, is a Chadic language spoken across at least 30 villages in Nigeria, though its speakers are gradually shifting to Hausa.

geruma.name: Geruma
geruma.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
geruma.description: Geruma, also called Gerema or Germa, is a Chadic language of Nigeria with Duurum and Sum dialects, whose speakers are shifting to Hausa.

ghotuo.name: Ghotuo
ghotuo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Niger > Edoid > North-Central Edoid > Ghotuo-Uneme-Yekhee
ghotuo.description: Ghotuo, also known as Otwa or Otuo, is a North Central Edoid language spoken mostly in the Owan and Akoko-Edo areas of Edo State, Nigeria.

giiwo.name: Giiwo
giiwo.description: Giiwo, also known as Bu Giiwo or Kirfi, is a Chadic language of the Afro-Asiatic family spoken in Nigeria.

gokana.name: Gokana
gokana.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Ogoni
gokana.description: Gokana is an Ogoni language spoken by roughly 130,000 people in Rivers State, Nigeria.

gubi.name: Shiki
gubi.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
gubi.description: Shiki, also known as Gubi, is one of the Jarawan Bantu languages of Nigeria, spoken in Bauchi State.

gudu.name: Gudu
gudu.description: Gudu, also known as Gudo or Gutu, is an Afro-Asiatic language spoken in the Song Local Government Area of Adamawa State, with Kumbi as one of its dialects.

guruntum.name: Guruntum
guruntum.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > South Bauchi
guruntum.description: Guruntum is a Chadic language spoken in the Bauchi and Alkaleri Local Government Areas of Bauchi State, with an estimated 15,000 speakers as of 1993.

gusu.name: Gusu
gusu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji > Shammo
gusu.description: Gusu, also known as Gussum or Baw, is an East Kainji language belonging to the Shammo cluster, spoken in Toro Local Government Area of Bauchi State and Bassa Local Government Area of Plateau State.

gwa.name: Gwa
gwa.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
gwa.description: Gwa is one of the Jarawan Bantu languages spoken in Nigeria, part of a small pocket of Bantu languages found outside the core Bantu-speaking region of Central and Southern Africa.

gwandara.name: Gwandara
gwandara.description: Gwandara is a West Chadic language and the closest known relative of Hausa, spoken in several dialects across north-central Nigeria by the Gwandara people, who number around 30,000 and are concentrated in Abuja, Niger, Kaduna, Kogi and Nasarawa States, including the resettlement town of New Karshi.

gwoza.name: Lamang
gwoza.description: Lamang, also spelled Laamang, is an Afro-Asiatic language cluster of Nigeria; researchers have classified its Woga (Gwoza) variety as a distinct language within the cluster.

gyem.name: Gyem
gyem.description: Gyem is a Kainji language spoken in Bauchi State, Nigeria.

hona.name: Hwana
hona.description: Hwana, also known as Hwona, Hona, Tuftera or Fiterya, is an Afro-Asiatic language spoken in Adamawa State, Nigeria.

humono.name: Humono
humono.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River
humono.description: Humono, also known as Ohumono or Bahumono, is a Central Cross River language spoken by the Bahumono people in Abi Local Government Area of Cross River State.

ichen.name: Izere
ichen.description: Izere is a dialect continuum of Plateau languages in Nigeria that some researchers analyze as four distinct languages, though Ethnologue does not separate its northwestern and northeastern varieties; its Cen dialect has picked up Berom-style noun-class prefixes and consonant alternation.

idesa.name: Okpe
idesa.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Niger > Edoid > Southwestern Edoid
idesa.description: Okpe is a Southwestern Edoid language of Nigeria spoken by the Urhobo people.

idon.name: Idon
idon.description: Idon, also known as Idong or Ajiya, is a Plateau language spoken in Nigeria.

ijumu.name: Ijumu
ijumu.description: The Ijumu people of Ijumu Local Government Area in Kogi State, centered on the town of Iyara, speak the Okun dialect of Yoruba.

iko.name: Iko
iko.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Lower Cross River
iko.description: Iko is a Lower Cross River language of Nigeria whose speakers are ethnically Obolo, though the Iko language itself is not the Obolo people's main language.

ikpeshi.name: Ikpeshi
ikpeshi.description: Ikpeshi is an Edoid language spoken in Edo State, Nigeria.

ikulu.name: Kulu
ikulu.description: Kulu, also known as Ikulu or Ankulu, is a Plateau language spoken by the Bakulu people in the Zangon Kataf, Kachia and Kauru Local Government Areas of Kaduna State.

irigwe.name: Rigwe
irigwe.description: Rigwe, also called Nkarigwe, is a Plateau language spoken by the Irigwe people, mainly in Bassa Local Government Area of Plateau State; it has a notably complex phonology, including a lateral fricative sound unusual among Plateau languages that suggests earlier contact with West Chadic languages in the area.

ivbie-north-okpela-arhe.name: Okpela
ivbie-north-okpela-arhe.description: Okpela, also spelled Okpella, is a dialect cluster of Edoid languages spoken in Nigeria.

iyala.name: Yala
iyala.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Idomoid > Akweya > Etulo-Idoma
iyala.description: Yala, also known as Iyala, is an Idomoid language spoken around Ogoja in Cross River State, Nigeria, with dialects including Ikom, Obubra, and Ogoja as classified by Blench in 2019.

izere.name: Izere
izere.description: Izere is a dialect continuum of Plateau languages in Nigeria that some researchers analyze as four distinct languages, though Ethnologue does not separate its northwestern and northeastern varieties; its Cen and Ganang varieties each have only around 2,000 speakers, and Cen has absorbed Berom-style noun-class prefixes and consonant alternation.

jaku.name: Labir
jaku.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
jaku.description: Labir, also known as Jaku, is one of the Jarawan Bantu languages of Nigeria, spoken in Bauchi State.

jara.name: Jara
jara.description: Jara, also known as Jera, is spoken in Borno and Gombe States across the Biu, Kwaya-Kusar, Akko, and Yamaltu-Deba Local Government Areas, and had about 46,000 speakers as of 2000; its use is declining as speakers shift to Fulfulde and Hausa.

jere.name: Zele
jere.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
jere.description: Zele, also known as Zelle, Jere, or Jera, is an East Kainji language spoken in Bassa Local Government Area in northern Plateau State, Nigeria.

kagoma.name: Gyong
kagoma.description: Gyong, also known as Kagoma, is a Plateau language spoken by about 20,000 people who call themselves Acipu, in Jema'a Local Government Area in southern Kaduna State.

kamantan.name: Ngyian
kamantan.description: Ngyian, known in Hausa as Kamantan, is a Plateau language spoken by the Anghan people of Nigeria.

kambari.name: Kambari
kambari.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > Shingini
kambari.description: The Kambari, or Kamberi, languages are a cluster of Kainji languages spoken in northwestern Nigeria, in Kebbi State and Niger State.

kamo.name: Kamo
kamo.description: Kamo, also known as Ma or Nyii Ma, is a Savannas language of Gombe State in eastern Nigeria; its speakers originally settled on Kamo Hill before gradually moving down into the plains during the 20th century.

kantana.name: Mama
kantana.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
kantana.description: Mama, also known as Kantana, is a Jarawan Bantu language spoken in Nigeria; a 1973 estimate gave 20,000 speakers, while a 2011 source recorded only 2,000-3,000 speakers under the name Kantana, mostly people over 25 years old.

karekare.name: Karai-karai
karekare.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
karekare.description: Karai-karai, also spelled Karekare or Kerrikerri, is an Afro-Asiatic language spoken by the ethnic Karai-Karai people, primarily in Bauchi, Yobe, and Gombe States in northeastern Nigeria, with an estimated 1.5 to 1.8 million speakers.

kariya.name: Kariya
kariya.description: Kariya, also known as Vinahe, is an Afroasiatic language spoken by the Wiihe people in villages near the Stone Age archaeological site of Kariya Wuro in Ganjuwa Local Government Area, Bauchi State; the Wiihe maintain a rich ritual culture that includes masquerades and a circumcision ceremony known as sar.

kentin.name: Yukubenic
kentin.description: The Yukubenic, or Oohum, languages form a small branch spoken in southeastern Nigeria whose position is disputed: Glottolog places it within the Plateau family, Ethnologue places it within Jukunoid, and Blench has proposed it as an independent branch of Benue-Congo.

kilba.name: Huba
kilba.description: Huba, also known as Nya Huba or Kilba, is a Chadic language spoken in Nigeria.

kinuku.name: Kinuku
kinuku.description: Kinuku, also known as Nu, is a Kainji language spoken in Nigeria.

kir-balar.name: Kir-Balar
kir-balar.description: Kir-Balar, also known as Kir or Kirr, is a pair of closely related Afro-Asiatic languages spoken in the villages of Kir Bengbet and Kir Bajang'le, in Bauchi Local Government Area, Bauchi State.

kirfi.name: Giiwo
kirfi.description: Giiwo, also known as Bu Giiwo, Kirfi, Kirifi, or Kirifawa, is a Chadic language spoken in Nigeria.

kofyar.name: Pan
kofyar.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Angas > Sura-Gerka
kofyar.description: Pan, commonly known as Kofyar, is an Afro-Asiatic dialect cluster spoken in Plateau State, Nigeria.

kona.name: Jiba
kona.description: Jiba, also known as Kona, is an endangered Jukunoid language of the Niger-Congo family spoken by about 2,000 people across Bauchi, Plateau, and Taraba States.

kubi.name: Kubi
kubi.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
kubi.description: Kubi, also known as Kuba or Kubawa, is an extinct Afro-Asiatic language formerly spoken in Bauchi State by members of the Za'ar tribe, who have since shifted to Hausa.

kugama.name: Kugama
kugama.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Adamawa-Ubangi > Leko-Nimbari > Mumuye-Yendang
kugama.description: Kugama, also known as Wam or Gengle, is an Adamawa language of the Yendang group spoken in the Mayo-Belwa and Fufore Local Government Areas of Adamawa State; speakers refer to their own language as nya wam.

kulere.name: Kulere
kulere.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Ron
kulere.description: Kulere, also known as Tof, Korom Boye, Akandi, or Akande, is an Afro-Asiatic language spoken in Bokkos, Plateau State, and in parts of Wamba, Nasarawa State, Nigeria.

kurama.name: Kurama
kurama.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
kurama.description: Kurama, also known as T'kurmi or Akurmi, is a Kainji language spoken across the north-central Nigerian states of Kaduna, Bauchi, Borno, Kano, Jigawa, and Plateau; about 88 percent of the Akurmi people are Christian.

kushi.name: Goji
kushi.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale > Tangalic
kushi.description: Goji, also known as Kushi or Chong'e, is a West Chadic language spoken in Nigeria.

kuteb.name: Kuteb
kuteb.description: Kuteb, also known as Kutep, Ati, Kutev, or Mbarike, is spoken mainly in the Takum and Ussa Local Government Areas and Yangtu Special Development Area of Taraba State, with a small number of speakers also found across the border in Cameroon.

kutin.name: Kutin
kutin.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Adamawa-Ubangi > Leko-Nimbari > Duru
kutin.description: Kutin belongs to the Duru branch of the Savannas languages; most of its Nigerian speakers relocated to Cameroon after the Gashaka-Gumti National Park was established.

kuturmi.name: Kuturmi
kuturmi.description: Kuturmi, also known as Ibiro-Ikryo or Ada, is a Plateau language cluster spoken in Kachia Local Government Area, Kaduna State, Nigeria.

kwanka.name: Kwanka
kwanka.description: Also known as Kadung or Kadun, Kwanka is a dialect cluster of Plateau languages spoken in central Nigeria.

kyenga.name: Kyenga
kyenga.description: Kyenga (also called Tyenga or Kyanga) is a Mande language spoken along the Nigeria-Benin border, though usage is declining as speakers shift to Hausa in Nigeria and Dendi in Benin.

lamja.name: Daka
lamja.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Northern Bantoid > Dakoid > Daka
lamja.description: Daka is one of two languages spoken by the Chamba people of Nigeria, alongside Chamba Leko, and belongs to the Dakoid branch of Bantoid.

lau.name: Lau
lau.description: Lau is a Jukunoid language spoken in Lau LGA, Taraba State, whose speakers claim mutual intelligibility with the Jukunoid varieties of Kunini, Bandawa, and Jeshi; it was previously misclassified as a Mbum language.

longuda.name: Longuda
longuda.description: Also known as Lunguda, this Niger-Congo language is spoken around the volcanic Lunguda Plateau in Adamawa State; Joseph Greenberg classed it as a distinct Adamawa branch, though later scholarship places it among the Bambukic languages.

lopa.name: Lopa
lopa.description: Also called Rop, Lopa is a minor Kainji language of Nigeria closely related to the Cuba language, with some speakers having shifted to the neighboring Busa language.

mabo.name: Barkul
mabo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Plateau > East Plateau
mabo.description: Barkul (also Bo-Rukul) is a Plateau language of Barkul village in Bokkos LGA, Plateau State, made up of two notably distinct dialects, Bo and Rukul, each with only a few hundred to about a thousand speakers, and appears to be most closely related to Fyam and Horom.

mama.name: Mama
mama.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Bantu > Mbam-Bubi-Jarawan > Jarawan
mama.description: Also known as Kantana, Mama is a Jarawan Bantu language of Nigeria whose speaker numbers have declined sharply, from an estimated 20,000 in 1973 to only a few thousand, mostly older speakers, by 2011.

mangas.name: Mantsi
mangas.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > South Bauchi
mangas.description: Also known as Ma'as or Mangas, Mantsi is an endangered Afro-Asiatic language spoken in Mangas town, Bauchi State, whose grammatical structure is reported to differ significantly from other South Bauchi languages.

mbembe.name: Mbembe
mbembe.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River
mbembe.description: Mbembe is a Cross River language of Nigeria; its divergent Odut variety, spoken in an outlying village, had only 20 known speakers as of 1980 and may now be extinct.

migili.name: Jili
migili.description: Also known as Lijili, Jili is a Plateau language of central Nigeria whose speakers were scattered across the region by 19th-century slave raids; it shares the ambiguous name "Koro" with several other unrelated languages.

miya.name: Miya
miya.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > Warji
miya.description: Miya (Miyawa) is a Chadic language spoken by around 5,000 people in Bauchi State and is related to Hausa, from which its speakers sometimes borrow vocabulary.

montol.name: Montol
montol.description: Spoken in Plateau State, Montol has Baltap-Lalin and Montol as its main dialects; linguist Roger Blench has also referred to it by the name Tel or Tehl.

mushere.name: Cakfem-Mushere
mushere.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Angas
mushere.description: Cakfem-Mushere is an Afro-Asiatic language cluster of Bokkos LGA, Plateau State, with Kadim-Kaban and Jajura dialects; it is very close to and highly mutually intelligible with Mwaghavul and Mupun.

ndoro.name: Ndoro
ndoro.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Northern Bantoid > Mambiloid
ndoro.description: Also known as Ndoola or, in Cameroon, Njoyame, Ndoro is a Bantoid language of Nigeria with several thousand additional speakers across the border in Cameroon, and is thought to be related to or part of the Mambiloid languages.

nembe.name: Nembe
nembe.description: Nembe is one of two dialects, alongside Akassa, that make up the Southeast Ijo language spoken in the southern Niger Delta of Nigeria.

ngamo.name: Ngamo
ngamo.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale
ngamo.description: Also called Ngamawa or Gamawa, Ngamo has two major dialects, Gudi Ngamo and Yaya Ngamo, and, as a member of the West Chadic branch, is related to the regionally dominant Hausa language.

nindem.name: Ninkyob-Nindem
nindem.description: Also known as Kaningkom-Nindem, Ninkyob-Nindem is a dialect cluster of Plateau languages spoken in central Nigeria.

nzanyi.name: Nzanyi
nzanyi.description: Nzanyi, also known by names such as Njanyi or Nzangi, is spoken across numerous dialects in Maiha LGA, Adamawa State, and by a smaller population just across the border in northern Cameroon, though it is mainly spoken in Nigeria.

ododop.name: Korop
ododop.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River
ododop.description: Also known as Ododop, Durop, or Erorop, Korop is an Upper Cross River language spoken in Nigeria.

ogba.name: Ogba
ogba.description: Ogba is an Igboid language spoken mostly in Rivers State by the Ogba people, who form part of the Ogba/Egbema/Ndoni Local Government Area and whose traditional ruler, the Eze-Ogba, was politically pressured to adopt the title "Oba" as used by the Benin monarchy.

ogbia.name: Ogbia
ogbia.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Central Delta
ogbia.description: Also known as AgBeya or Abaya, Ogbia is the most widely spoken Central Delta language of Nigeria, with over 200,000 speakers across varieties including Kolo and Oloibiri.

okobo.name: Okobo
okobo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Lower Cross River > Obolo
okobo.description: Okobo is a Lower Cross River language of Nigeria, closely grouped with Obolo within the Lower Cross subgroup.

olulumo.name: Ikom
olulumo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River
olulumo.description: Ikom is an Upper Cross River language of Nigeria with three varieties, Okuni, Lulumo (Olulumo), and Ikom proper, the last of which accounts for about 80 percent of speakers; it is sometimes called by the combined name Olulumo-Ikom.

oworo.name: Oworo
oworo.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Kwa > Edekiri > Ede > Eastern Ede > Southeastern Ede > Nuclear Yoruba > Yoruba > Central Yoruba
oworo.description: Also spelled Aworo, Oworo is a Yoruba dialect spoken mainly in the Oworo District of Lokoja LGA, Kogi State, and belongs with the mutually intelligible Okun dialect group alongside Owe, Yagba, Ijumu, and Bunu; it is notable for regularly shifting the Yoruba [f] sound to [h].

paa.name: Pa'a
paa.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > Warji
paa.description: Also known as Afa or by the autonym Fucaka, Pa'a is a Chadic language spoken in Bauchi State, Nigeria.

panyam.name: Mak
panyam.description: Also called LeeMak, Mak is one of several languages spoken by the Bikwin people of Karim Lamido LGA, Taraba State, with two dialects, Panya and Zoo, that are divergent enough to potentially be considered separate languages.

pero.name: Pero
pero.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Bole-Tangale > Tangalic
pero.description: Pero is a West Chadic language spoken in Nigeria, part of the Tangalic branch alongside languages such as Tangale.

piti.name: Piti
piti.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
piti.description: Also known as Bishi, Piti is a minor Kainji language spoken in at least 26 villages of Kaduna State; the related Ngmgbang variety was once classified as a Bishi dialect but is now considered a distinct, closely related language.

polchi.name: Polci
polchi.classification: Afro-Asiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > South Bauchi
polchi.description: Also spelled Pelci or Pose, Polci is an Afro-Asiatic language of Bauchi State belonging to the Barawa cluster within the West Chadic family.

pongu.name: Pongu
pongu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > Shiroro
pongu.description: Also called Pangu or Rin, Pongu is a Kainji language with about 20,000 speakers centered on Pangu Gari town in Niger State, southeast of Tegina.

pyapun.name: Pyapun
pyapun.description: Pyapun is an Afro-Asiatic language spoken in about ten villages east of the Panyam-Shendam road in Plateau State, Nigeria.

rebina.name: Bunu
rebina.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji > Shammo
rebina.description: Also known as Ribina or Rebina, Bunu is an East Kainji language spoken in Toro LGA of Bauchi State, belonging to the Shammo cluster of related Kainji languages.

reshe.name: Reshe
reshe.description: Reshe is considered the most divergent of the Kainji languages, spoken on both shores of Kainji Lake in Yauri LGA, Kebbi State, and Borgu LGA, Niger State. Birnin Yauri, near Yelwa, is regarded as the ancient city of the Reshe people.

ron.name: Ron
ron.classification: Afroasiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic A > Ron
ron.description: Ron, also called Challa or Chala, is a cluster of Afro-Asiatic (Chadic) dialects spoken in Plateau State, including the Bokkos, Daffo-Mbar-Butura, and Monguna varieties, some of which linguists consider separate languages in their own right.

rukuba.name: Ce
rukuba.description: The Ce language, also called Che or Kuce, is a regionally significant Plateau language of Nigeria, also known by the name of its native district, Rukuba, in Plateau State.

ruma.name: Ruma
ruma.description: Ruma, also known as Ruruma, is a Kainji language spoken in Nigeria, part of the broader Kainji subgroup of Benue-Congo languages found across the country's Middle Belt.

saya.name: Saya
saya.classification: Afroasiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > South Bauchi
saya.description: Saya, also known as Sayanci or Zaar, is a cluster of Chadic dialects spoken in Nigeria, forming part of the South Bauchi group of West Chadic languages.

shamang.name: Shamang
shamang.description: Shamang is a poorly documented Plateau language of Nigeria, reflecting the limited linguistic research available on many of the small Plateau-branch languages of the Middle Belt.

shanga.name: Shanga
shanga.description: Shanga, also known as Shangawa or Shonga, is a Mande language spoken in Nigeria, making it one of the few representatives of the Mande branch of Niger-Congo found within the country.

sheni.name: Sheni-Ziriya
sheni.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji > Ziriya
sheni.description: Sheni (Shaini) and Ziriya (Jiriya) together form a single East Kainji language of Nigeria; the two communities are geographically separate but may not be linguistically distinct.

shoo-minda-nye.name: Shoo-Minda-Nye
shoo-minda-nye.description: Shoo, Minda, and Nye are three dialects that together make up a single unnamed Jukunoid language of Nigeria; the Minda dialect is spoken widely in what is now Lau LGA, Taraba State.

siri.name: Siri
siri.classification: Afroasiatic > Chadic > West Chadic > West Chadic B > Warji
siri.description: Siri, also known as Sirawa, is a highly endangered Afro-Asiatic language of the Warji group spoken in Bauchi State, Nigeria.

sukur.name: Sukur
sukur.description: Sukur, also called Adikimmu Sukur or Sakun, is a Biu-Mandara (Chadic) language spoken in Madagali LGA of Adamawa State, Nigeria.

surubu.name: Vori
surubu.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Kainji > East Kainji
surubu.description: Vori, also called Surubu or Skrubu, is a Kainji language of Nigeria centered on the town of Geshere, west of Mariri on the Lere road, with speakers spread across roughly 32 settlements.

teme.name: Teme
teme.description: Teme is an Adamawa-branch Niger-Congo language spoken in Nigeria, part of the group of Adamawa languages concentrated in the country's northeast.

tera.name: Tera
tera.description: Tera is a Chadic dialect cluster spoken in northeastern Nigeria, across the northern and eastern parts of Gombe and Borno States; its Pidlimdi (Hinna) dialect has been argued to be a distinct language.

teshena.name: Teshenawa
teshena.description: Teshenawa, or Teshenanchi, is an extinct Afro-Asiatic language once spoken in Teshena town, Kafin Hausa LGA, Jigawa State, and was closely related to the also-extinct Auyokawa as well as the still-spoken Bade language.

tigon.name: Tigon Mbembe
tigon.description: Tigon Mbembe, a form of Mbembe, is a Jukunoid language spoken across the northern Nigeria-Cameroon border region.

tikar.name: Tikar
tikar.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid
tikar.description: Tikar is a Northern Bantoid, semi-Bantu language spoken by the Tikar people as well as the Bedzan Pygmies, who use their own dialect of it; some linguists consider Tikar a divergent Niger-Congo language of uncertain origin.

tula.name: Tula
tula.description: Tula, also known as Kotule or Kitule, is one of the Savanna-branch languages of Gombe State in northeastern Nigeria.

ufia.name: Orring
ufia.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River
ufia.description: Orring, also known as Korring, is an Upper Cross River language spoken by the Orring people, who live across Benue, Cross River, and Ebonyi States in Nigeria.

ukelle.name: Kele
ukelle.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River
ukelle.description: Kele, also called Kukelle, is an Upper Cross River language spoken mainly in Yala LGA of Cross River State; its Ukelle speakers are divided into North and South Ukelle communities distinguished chiefly by intonation.

uneme.name: Uneme
uneme.description: Uneme is an Edoid language spoken in Edo State, Nigeria, belonging to the same Edoid family that includes Edo and other related languages of the region.

uvwie.name: Uvwie
uvwie.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Niger > Edoid > Southwestern Edoid
uvwie.description: Uvwie, also called Ephron or Effurun, is a Niger Delta language spoken by the Uvwie people of southern Nigeria, classified as a co-ordinate member of the Southwestern Edoid branch alongside Urhobo, Okpe, and Isoko.

uyanga.name: Uyanga
uyanga.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River > Agoi-Doko-Iyoniyong
uyanga.description: Uyanga, also known as Doko or Iko, is a minor Upper Cross River language spoken in Nigeria.

verre.name: Vere
verre.description: The Vere language, also known as Gweri, Kobo, or Mom Jango, belongs to the Duru branch of the Savanna languages and is spoken across the northern border area between Nigeria and Cameroon.

vono.name: Vono
vono.description: Vono, also known as Kiwollo or Kiballo, is a nearly extinct Kainji language spoken in Nigeria.

waka.name: Waka
waka.description: Waka is an Adamawa-branch language of Nigeria, part of the cluster of small Adamawa languages spoken in the country's northeast.

warji.name: Warji
warji.description: Warji, also called Warjawa or Sirzakwai, is an Afro-Asiatic (Chadic) language spoken in Bauchi State, Nigeria, whose speakers are increasingly shifting to Hausa.

yace.name: Yace
yace.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Idomoid > Yatye-Akpa
yace.description: Yace, also spelled Yache, is an Idomoid language of Cross River State, Nigeria, spoken in Yala LGA across communities including Alifokpa, Imbuo, and Osina, and is closely related to the Akpa language.

yakurr.name: Yakö
yakurr.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Cross River > Upper Cross River > Loko
yakurr.description: The Yakö language, also known as Lokö or Lokạạ, is an Upper Cross River language spoken by the Yakö (Yakurr) people of Nigeria.

yandang.name: Yendang
yandang.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Adamawa-Ubangi > Leko-Nimbari > Mumuye-Yendang
yandang.description: Yendang belongs to the Leko-Nimbari group of Savanna languages and is spoken in northeastern Nigeria, with dialects including Kuseki, Yofo, and Poli.

zhire.name: Zhire
zhire.description: Zhire, known as Zhebzhi in Hyam and Kenyi in Hausa, is a poorly documented Plateau language of Nigeria; a variety called Shang is a relexified form of Zhire.

awori.name: Awori
awori.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Defoid > Yoruboid > Edekiri > Yoruba
awori.description: The Awori are a subgroup of the Yoruba people based in Lagos State, and they speak their own dialect of the Yoruba language.

bajju.name: Bajju (Jju)
bajju.description: Spoken by the Bajju people of the Middle Belt, chiefly in the southern part of Kaduna State around Kachia, Zangon Kataf, and Jema'a, the Jju language gives the Bajju their name, from "Banyet Jju" meaning people who speak Jju.

ejagham.name: Ejagham
ejagham.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Benue-Congo > Bantoid > Southern Bantoid > Ekoid
ejagham.description: Also known as Ekoi, Ejagham is an Ekoid language spoken by the Ekoi people across the Nigeria-Cameroon border region. The Ekoi are among the peoples credited with using and possibly creating the Nsibidi ideographic writing system.

mwana.name: Dikaka
mwana.classification: Niger-Congo > Atlantic-Congo > Volta-Congo > Adamawa > Waja-Longuda > Dijim-Bwilim
mwana.description: Also called Cham or Dijim-Bwilim after its two dialects Dijim and Bwilim, Dikaka is a tonal Middle Belt language with a whistled register, spoken in Gombe State and the southwestern parts of Adamawa State.
